sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Bladder cancer typically presents with microscopic or gross hematuria, which is the most common initial symptom. Hematuria may be painless and intermittent, often leading to delayed diagnosis. Urinary frequency, urgency, and dysuria due to bladder irritation or reduced capacity are also common. A subset of patients may initially present with symptoms mimicking a urinary tract infection. In more advanced disease, upper tract obstruction or flank pain can occur due to tumor extension or ureteral involvement. The median age at diagnosis is 73 years, and the disease is rare in individuals under 40 years [2].

symptoms.name: Hematuria
symptoms.detail: Microscopic or gross painless hematuria; the most frequent presenting symptom.
symptoms.frequency: Most common; present in the majority of patients at diagnosis.

symptoms.name: Urinary frequency/urgency
symptoms.detail: Symptoms of bladder irritation or reduced bladder capacity.

symptoms.name: Dysuria
symptoms.detail: Painful urination, often associated with infection or irritative symptoms.

symptoms.name: Urinary tract infection symptoms
symptoms.detail: May mimic or coexist with bladder cancer, especially in older adults.
symptoms.frequency: Less common as an isolated presentation.

symptoms.name: Obstructive symptoms
symptoms.detail: Flank pain, hydronephrosis, or renal failure due to ureteral obstruction by tumor.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; indicates advanced or obstructing tumor.

alarms.name: Hematuria
alarms.detail: Persistent or gross hematuria warrants urgent investigation.

alarms.name: Urinary frequency/urgency
alarms.detail: Persistent symptoms without infection are concerning.

alarms.name: Dysuria
alarms.detail: If persistent without evidence of infection, consider bladder pathology.

alarms.name: Urinary tract infection symptoms
alarms.detail: Recurrent or refractory UTI-like symptoms should prompt cystoscopy.

alarms.name: Obstructive symptoms
alarms.detail: Obstructive symptoms require urgent evaluation for metastatic or locally advanced disease.
